<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:五重塔]][[ko:오층탑]][[zh:五重塔]]
{{Source citation|date=December 2021}} {{Other uses|Buddhist Pagoda|Koda Rohan's Novel|Five-storied Pagoda (novel)}} '''Five-storied Pagoda''' (五重塔) is one of the forms of {{ill|Stupa|en|Stupa|qid=Q180987|lt=Stupa|1=Stupa}}. It refers to the {{ill|Stupa|en|Stupa|qid=Q180987|lt=Tiered Pagoda|1=Stupa}} – that is, the pavilion‐style pagoda which possesses a roof with {{ill|AD 5|en|AD 5|qid=Q19610|lt=5|1=AD 5}} tiers. It consists, from the bottom, of Earth (foundation), Water (pagoda body), Fire (hat), Wind (canopy flower) and Void (treasure jewel); each element represents one of the five worlds ({{ill|Godai (Japanese philosophy)|en|Godai (Japanese philosophy)|qid=Q3109675|lt=Five Great Elements|1=Godai (Japanese philosophy)}}) and expresses a Buddhist {{ill|Cosmology|en|Cosmology|qid=Q338|lt=Cosmology|1=Cosmology}}.
<gallery> File:Horyu-ji06s3200.jpg|Horyu-ji Pagoda: World's oldest five-storied pagoda File:Toji-temple-kyoto.jpg|To-ji Pagoda: Japan's largest five-storied pagoda, Edo period File:Murou-ji 4.jpg|Murō-ji Pagoda: A small pagoda from the late Nara to early Heian period File:Daigoji Kyoto02bs4200.jpg|Daigo-ji Pagoda: Pagoda from the Heian period File:Kofukuji08s3200.jpg|Kōfuku-ji Pagoda: Japan's second tallest pagoda. Muromachi period File:Five tier pagoda at Mt. Haguro 2006-10-29.jpg|Haguro-san Five-storied Pagoda: The oldest five-storied pagoda in the Tōhoku region (pillar erected in 1369 – sōrin set atop in 1377) File:Myō-ō-in (Fukuyama), Five-storied Pagoda 001.jpg|Myōō-in Pagoda: The oldest five-storied pagoda in the Chūgoku region (1348) </gallery>
{{Source citation|date=December 2021}} {{Other uses|Buddhist Pagoda|Koda Rohan's Novel|Five-storied Pagoda (novel)}} '''Five-storied Pagoda''' (五重塔) is one of the forms of {{ill|Stupa|en|Stupa|qid=Q180987|lt=Stupa|1=Stupa}}. It refers to the {{ill|Stupa|en|Stupa|qid=Q180987|lt=Tiered Pagoda|1=Stupa}} – that is, the pavilion‐style pagoda which possesses a roof with {{ill|AD 5|en|AD 5|qid=Q19610|lt=5|1=AD 5}} tiers. It consists, from the bottom, of Earth (foundation), Water (pagoda body), Fire (hat), Wind (canopy flower) and Void (treasure jewel); each element represents one of the five worlds ({{ill|Godai (Japanese philosophy)|en|Godai (Japanese philosophy)|qid=Q3109675|lt=Five Great Elements|1=Godai (Japanese philosophy)}}) and expresses a Buddhist {{ill|Cosmology|en|Cosmology|qid=Q338|lt=Cosmology|1=Cosmology}}.
{{右| [[File:Horyu-ji06s3200.jpg|thumb|none|180px|{{ill|Horyu-ji Pagoda|de|Horyu-ji Pagoda|ja|法隆寺塔|lt=Horyu-ji Pagoda|lt_de=Horyu-ji Pagoda|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Horyu-ji Pagoda|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}: World's Oldest Five-storied Pagoda]] [[File:Toji-temple-kyoto.jpg|thumb|none|180px|{{ill|Tō-ji Pagoda|de|Tō-ji Pagoda|ja|東寺塔|lt=Tō-ji Pagoda|lt_de=Tō-ji Pagoda|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tō-ji Pagoda|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}: Japan's Largest Five-storied Pagoda, Edo Period]] [[File:Murou-ji 4.jpg|thumb|none|180px|{{ill|Murō-ji Pagoda|de|Murō-ji Pagoda|ja|室生寺塔|lt=Murō-ji Pagoda|lt_de=Murō-ji Pagoda|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murō-ji Pagoda|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}: A small pagoda from the late Nara to early Heian period]] [[File:Daigoji Kyoto02bs4200.jpg|180px|thumb|none|{{ill|Daigo-ji Pagoda|de|Daigo-ji Pagoda|ja|醍醐寺塔|lt=Daigo-ji Pagoda|lt_de=Daigo-ji Pagoda|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daigo-ji Pagoda|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}: Pagoda from the Heian Period]] [[File:Kofukuji08s3200.jpg|thumb|none|180px|{{ill|Kōfuku-ji Pagoda|de|Kōfuku-ji Pagoda|ja|興福寺塔|lt=Kōfuku-ji Pagoda|lt_de=Kōfuku-ji Pagoda|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōfuku-ji Pagoda|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}: Japan's 2nd Tallest Pagoda, Muromachi Period]] [[File:Five tier pagoda at Mt. Haguro 2006-10-29.jpg|thumb|none|180px|{{ill|Hagurosan Five-storied Pagoda|de|Hagurosan Fünfstöckige Pagode|ja|羽黒山五重塔|lt=Hagurosan Five-storied Pagoda|lt_de=Hagurosan Fünfstöckige Pagode|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hagurosan Five-storied Pagoda|1=Five-storied Pagoda, Haguro-san|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11610424}}: Oldest Five-storied Pagoda in the Tohoku region ({{ill|1369|en|1369|qid=Q6211|lt=1369|1=1369}} pillar – {{ill|1377|en|1377|qid=Q6289|lt=1377|1=1377}} finial))]] [[File:Myō-ō-in (Fukuyama), Five-storied Pagoda 001.jpg|thumb|none|180px|{{ill|Myō-ō-in Pagoda|de|Myō-ō-in Pagoda|ja|明王院塔|lt=Myō-ō-in Pagoda|lt_ja=明王院塔|lt_de=Myō-ō-in Pagoda|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myō-ō-in Pagoda|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}: Oldest Five-storied Pagoda in the Chugoku region ({{ill|1348|en|1348|qid=Q6085|lt=1348|1=1348}}))]] }}
== Overview == Buddhist pagodas originated from the stupas built around the 3rd century BCE in ancient {{ill|India|en|India|qid=Q668|lt=India|1=India}} to enshrine {{ill|Śarīra|en|Śarīra|qid=Q1156436|lt=Śarīra|1=Śarīra}} (the remains of the Buddha). The ancient Indian stupas were dome‐shaped (hemispherical), but when this form was transmitted to China, it adopted the style of pavilion architecture and became tall. These pavilion‐style tiered pagodas were transmitted to Japan via the Korean Peninsula. Wooden tiered pagodas remain abundant in Japan, whereas such examples in China and on the Korean Peninsula are extremely rare.
In Japan, many Buddhist temples and shrines feature wooden five‐storied or three‐storied pagodas, many of which serve as local {{ill|Landmark|en|Landmark|qid=Q4895393|lt=Landmark|1=Landmark}}. In addition to wooden pagodas, there are also pagodas constructed of stone, tile, or iron, and from modern times onward, pagodas built of reinforced concrete. Other multi-tiered pagodas include {{ill|Seven-storied Pagoda|de|Siebenstöckige Pagode|ja|七重塔|lt=Seven-storied Pagoda|lt_de=Siebenstöckige Pagode|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seven-storied Pagoda|qq=}}, nine‐storied and thirteen‐storied pagodas (the number of tiers is almost always odd); however, no wooden seven‐storied or nine‐storied pagodas from before the early modern period survive. In Nara Prefecture, at {{ill|Tanzan Shrine|en|Tanzan Shrine|qid=Q488618|lt=Dansan Shrine|1=Tanzan Shrine}}, there is a wooden thirteen‐storied pagoda; however, this is not a pavilion‐style tiered pagoda, as the roofs from the second to the thirteenth tier overlap densely with almost no space between them, and is classified as an eaves pagoda.

Fünfstöckige Pagode|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hagurosan Five-storied Pagoda|1=Five-storied Pagoda, Haguro-san|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11610424}} === Municipal Designation === * Reikōin Five-storied Small Pagoda – Located at Iwaya Sanzōin in {{ill|Yamaguchi Prefecture|en|Yamaguchi Prefecture|qid=Q127264|lt=Yamaguchi Prefecture|1=Yamaguchi Prefecture}}. In 1822, the construction of a five-storied pagoda was planned within the grounds of a Tenmangu Shrine. The master carpenter Matsuya Ki'emon produced a model, but construction was halted due to a peasant revolt and was never completed.

==== Five-storied Small Pagodas ==== * Suwa Taisha Five-storied Pagoda – {{ill|Shōwa era|en|Shōwa era|qid=Q327567|lt=Shōwa|1=Shōwa era}} 43 ({{ill|1968|en|1968|qid=Q2644|lt=1968|1=1968}}) * {{ill|Kitayama Honmon-ji Five-storied Pagoda|de|Kitayama Honmon-ji Five-storied Pagoda|ja|北山本門寺五重塔|lt=Kitayama Honmon-ji Five-storied Pagoda|lt_de=Kitayama Honmon-ji Five-storied Pagoda|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitayama Honmon-ji Five-storied Pagoda|1=Kitayama Honmon-ji Five-storied Pagoda|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q17211402}}
== Partially Surviving Five-storied Pagodas == * {{ill|Seijū-ji|de|Seijū-ji|ja|聖寿寺|lt=Seijū-ji|lt_de=Seijū-ji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seijū-ji|1=Shōjū-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11610977}} (Morioka City) Jizō Hall – the first tier has been repurposed as a Buddhist hall and survives. * {{ill|Tianning Temple|en|Tianning Temple|qid=Q2431159|lt=Tennen-ji|1=Tianning Temple}} (Onomichi City) three-storied pagoda – missing its upper portion, it survives as a three-storied pagoda.
== Non-Extant Five-storied Pagodas == === Notable Pagoda Ruins === * {{ill|Gangō-ji|en|Gangō-ji|qid=Q1203099|lt=Gango-ji|1=Gangō-ji}} – A five-storied pagoda, 72.7m tall{{Citation needed|date=November 2011}}, built in the Nara Period, was destroyed by fire in the late Edo period (Ansei 6, {{ill|1859|en|1859|qid=Q7687|lt=1859|1=1859}}). * {{ill|Tōshōdai-ji|en|Tōshōdai-ji|qid=Q973175|lt=Tōshōdai-ji|1=Tōshōdai-ji}} – An eastern pagoda built in the first year of the Kōnin era ({{ill|810|en|810|qid=Q23819|lt=810|1=810}}) was destroyed by lightning in Kyōwa 2 ({{ill|1802|en|1802|qid=Q6883|lt=1802|1=1802}}). * {{ill|Tachibana-dera|en|Tachibana-dera|qid=Q11544260|lt=Kitsujō-ji|1=Tachibana-dera}} – The ruins of a pagoda destroyed by lightning remain. * {{ill|Dōmyō-ji Tenmangu|de|Dōmyō-ji Tenmangu|ja|道明寺天満宮|lt=Dōmyō-ji Tenmangu|lt_de=Dōmyō-ji Tenmangu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dōmyō-ji Tenmangu|1=Dōmyōji Tenmangū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11641744}} – In Tenshō 3 ({{ill|1575|en|1575|qid=Q6688|lt=1575|1=1575}}), it was destroyed during the Battle of Takayashiro led by Oda Nobunaga; only the foundation stones remain. * {{ill|Tenmyōkoku-ji|de|Tenmyōkoku-ji|ja|天妙国寺|lt=Tenmyōkoku-ji|lt_de=Tenmyōkoku-ji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmyōkoku-ji|1=Tenmyōkoku-ji Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11442516}} – The ruins of a pagoda that was burned down in a great fire during the Genroku period remain. * {{ill|Tennō-ji (Taitō Ward)|de|Tennō-ji (Taitō Ward)|ja|天王寺|lt=Tennō-ji|lt_de=Tennō-ji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tennō-ji (Taitō Ward)|1=Tennōji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q10940450}} (Yanaka Tennō-ji) – A "Yanaka Five-storied Pagoda", rebuilt in {{ill|1791|en|1791|qid=Q7784|lt=1791|1=1791}}, was destroyed in the Yanaka Five-storied Pagoda Arson-Suicide Incident in {{ill|1957|en|1957|qid=Q5311|lt=1957|1=1957}}. * {{ill|Kunōzan Tōshō-gū|en|Kunōzan Tōshō-gū|qid=Q712162|lt=Kununoyama Tōshō-gū|1=Kunōzan Tōshō-gū}} – A pagoda established in 1636 ({{ill|Kanei 13|de|Kanei 13|ja|寛永13年|lt=Kanei 13|lt_de=Kanei 13|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanei 13|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) was demolished in 1873 (Meiji 6); it is said to have been 29.3m tall. * {{ill|Kitayama Honmon-ji Five-storied Pagoda|de|Kitayama Honmon-ji Five-storied Pagoda|ja|北山本門寺五重塔|lt=Kitayama Honmon-ji Five-storied Pagoda|lt_de=Kitayama Honmon-ji Five-storied Pagoda|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitayama Honmon-ji Five-storied Pagoda|1=Kitayama Honmon-ji Five-storied Pagoda|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q17211402}} (Honmon-ji) – The ruins of a pagoda destroyed by fire remain.
=== Others === * {{ill|Myōken-ji|de|Myōken-ji|ja|妙顕寺|lt=Myōken-ji|lt_de=Myōken-ji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōken-ji|1=Myōken-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11447081}} (Kyoto City) – Destroyed in the great Tenmei fire. * {{ill|Honkoku-ji|de|Honkoku-ji|ja|本圀寺|lt=Honkoku-ji|lt_de=Honkoku-ji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Honkoku-ji|1=Honkoku-ji Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11093340}} – Destroyed in the great Tenmei fire. * {{ill|Suwa-taisha|en|Suwa-taisha|qid=Q218813|lt=Suwa Taisha|1=Suwa-taisha}} (Kamisuwa Shrine) – Demolished during the separation of Shinto and Buddhism. * {{ill|Ōtori taisha|en|Ōtori taisha|qid=Q705151|lt=Ōtori Taisha|1=Ōtori taisha}} – Demolished during the separation of Shinto and Buddhism. * {{ill|Ōsu Kannon|en|Ōsu Kannon|qid=Q3594052|lt=Ōsu Kannon|1=Ōsu Kannon}} – Destroyed by fire in Meiji 25 (1892). * {{ill|Yakō-ji|de|Yakō-ji|ja|薬王寺|lt=Yakō-ji|lt_de=Yakō-ji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yakō-ji|1=Yakuōji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11622487}} (Kamakura City) – Destroyed by fire in Kyōhō 5 (1720). * {{ill|Ōbutsu-ji Five-storied Pagoda|de|Ōbutsu-ji Five-storied Pagoda|ja|応物寺五重塔|lt=Ōbutsu-ji Five-storied Pagoda|lt_de=Ōbutsu-ji Five-storied Pagoda|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōbutsu-ji Five-storied Pagoda|1=Ōmotsu-ji Five-storied Pagoda|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11491270}} (Ōbutsu-ji) – Collapsed in a typhoon in Taishō 2 (1912); it was the smallest outdoor five-storied pagoda in Japan. * {{ill|Zōjō-ji Five-storied Pagoda|de|Zōjō-ji Five-storied Pagoda|ja|増上寺五重塔|lt=Zōjō-ji Five-storied Pagoda|lt_de=Zōjō-ji Five-storied Pagoda|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zōjō-ji Five-storied Pagoda|1=Five-storied Pagoda, Zōjō-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11429124}} (Zōjō-ji) – Along with the {{ill|Sensō-ji Five-storied Pagoda|de|Sensō-ji Five-storied Pagoda|ja|浅草寺五重塔|lt=Sensō-ji Five-storied Pagoda|lt_de=Sensō-ji Five-storied Pagoda|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sensō-ji Five-storied Pagoda|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (reconstructed post-war in a relocated position), destroyed in the Tokyo air raids. * {{ill|Mohara-ji|de|Mohara-ji|ja|藻原寺|lt=Mohara-ji|lt_de=Mohara-ji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mohara-ji|1=Sōgen-ji Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11625126}} – No longer extant, but its existence is confirmed from Edo-period illustrations along with its soshidō.
== Outside Japan == * {{ill|Hōō-ji|de|Hōō-ji|ja|法住寺|lt=Hōō-ji|lt_de=Hōō-ji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōō-ji|1=Hōjū-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q64442717}} {{ill|Sassō-den|de|Sassō-den|ja|捌相殿|lt=Sassō-den|lt_de=Sassō-den|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sassō-den|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} – In Korea at {{ill|Songnisan|en|Songnisan|qid=Q482720|lt=Sokryeosan|1=Songnisan}}, rebuilt in 1626, 22m; in Korea, it is the oldest extant and only wooden five-storied pagoda (National Treasure) * {{ill|Buddhist Palace Pagoda|de|Buddhist Palace Pagoda|ja|仏宮寺釈迦塔|lt=Buddhist Palace Pagoda|lt_de=Buddhist Palace Pagoda|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Buddhist Palace Pagoda|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 応県木塔|1=Pagoda of Fogong Temple|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q117697}} ({{ill|Pagoda of Fogong Temple|en|Pagoda of Fogong Temple|qid=Q117697|lt=Ōken Mokutō|1=Pagoda of Fogong Temple}}) – In China, in {{ill|Shanxi|en|Shanxi|qid=Q46913|lt=Shanxi Province|1=Shanxi}}, built in 1056, 67m; its exterior appears to have 5 tiers but internally has 9 – the world's largest and oldest octagonal wooden pagoda.
== On the Seismic Resistance of Five-storied Pagodas == The seismic structure of five-storied pagodas, known as {{ill|Flexible Structure|de|Flexible Structure|ja|柔構造|lt=Flexible Structure|lt_de=Flexible Structure|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Flexible Structure|1=Flexible Structure|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11533931}}, has recently been adopted in skyscrapers worldwide as well as in Japan. However, the claim that traditional Japanese five-storied pagodas possess high seismic resistance is based on the fact that there are no historical records of their collapse due to earthquakes; thus, it remains a result-based inference, and it is uncertain whether they were intentionally designed with a flexible structure. Rather, considering the role of these pagodas as stupas enshrining the Buddha's relics, their design was primarily intended for religious symbolism, with the flexible structure emerging as a secondary byproduct. In ancient pagodas such as those at Hōryū-ji, despite the inevitable corrosion of the core column, only the core column is embedded in the ground as a set column, and unlike castle architecture, no structural beam ({{ill|nuki|de|nuki|ja|貫柱|lt=nuki|lt_de=nuki|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/nuki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) is passed through—resulting in an apparently irrational structural design.
The key term in the seismic resistance of five-storied pagodas is the "core column," yet the structure does not actually depend on it. Moreover, because the interior space was not intended for use, the density of the structural materials is high, rendering the building sturdier than typical constructions. In fact, as seen in the current Hōryū-ji pagoda—where the core column is corroded and not in contact with the ground—the structure remains sound even in the absence of the core column. Several theories have been proposed to explain the high seismic resistance: * Core Column Vibration Absorption Theory ::It was once widely held that because the core column running through the center of the pagoda is not fixed to the ground or lower floors, it absorbs the vibrations of each tier. However, such a structure is a construction method developed during the Edo period and does not explain older pagodas. Even in the case of a suspended core column, the installation of a thick anti-slip peg at the base leaves room for doubt regarding its pendulum-like damping effect. * The Latch (Kannuki) Theory ::This theory posits that the core column acts like a "latch" that secures doors, restraining the sway. However, since the core column comes into contact with surrounding members and may even damage them, an extremely sophisticated design would be necessary for the latch effect to function solely in seismic damping; in practice, its effect appears to be subject to chance.
Although many aspects of the seismic resistance remain unexplained, recent engineering studies—led by a group of structural researchers known as the "Group that Shakes the Five-storied Pagoda"—have conducted vibration experiments using one-fifth scale models, and in 2006 comparative experiments with and without the core column were performed. The results indicated that under tremors comparable to the {{ill|Hanshin-Awaji Earthquake|de|Hanshin-Awaji Erdbeben|ja|阪神・淡路大震災|lt=Hanshin-Awaji Earthquake|lt_de=Hanshin-Awaji Erdbeben|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hanshin-Awaji Earthquake|1=Damage by Great Hanshin earthquake|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11392882}}, the core column does help suppress the deformation and sway of the pagoda, yet no latch effect was observed; regardless of the presence of the core column, the seismic resistance is extremely high.<ref>[https://sciencechannel.jst.go.jp/B075101/detail/B075101012.html Science Channel, JST: (12) Shaking the Five-storied Pagoda (Part II) – The Secret of 1400 Years Without Collapse]</ref> However, it remains unclear whether the core column would exhibit a latch effect in an earthquake exceeding the magnitude of the Hanshin-Awaji Earthquake. Moreover, the primary reason for the high seismic resistance is the building's great height, which gives it a naturally long period of oscillation that does not easily coincide with the frequency of an earthquake.
Incidentally, the design firm {{ill|Tokyo Skytree|en|Tokyo Skytree|qid=Q57965|lt=Tokyo Skytree|1=Tokyo Skytree}}’s {{ill|Nikken Sekkei|en|Nikken Sekkei|qid=Q2804130|lt=Nikken Sekkei|1=Nikken Sekkei}} noted that the shape and configuration of their seismic damping system resembled that of a pagoda’s core column, and by analogy, dubbed it "core column damping." This led the media to report that pagoda technology had been applied; however, as stated above, many aspects of the pagoda's seismic resistance remain unexplained, and in practice modern seismic damping employs a "mass damper mechanism."
== Types of Core Column Structures == Five-storied pagoda core column designs have evolved over time and are generally classified as follows: * Excavated-type: The core column is erected embedded in the ground (e.g., at Hōryū-ji). This is the oldest type. * Foundation Stone-type: The core column is erected upon a foundation stone (e.g., the eastern pagoda of Yakushiji, Daigo-ji). * Starting from an Intermediate Tier: The core column is erected beginning on a middle tier (e.g., at Myō-ō-in, Kaijusan-ji). * Suspended-type: The core column is suspended from the pagoda by chains (e.g., at Nikkō Tōshō-gū); this type has been constructed since the Edo period.
== Others == * The Meiji-era writer {{ill|Kōda Rohan|en|Kōda Rohan|qid=Q1038466|lt=Koda Rohan|1=Kōda Rohan}}’s representative work is the novel {{ill|The Five-Storied Pagoda|en|The Five-Storied Pagoda|qid=Q7734263|lt=Five-storied Pagoda (novel)|1=The Five-Storied Pagoda}}, which depicts the life of carpenter Jūbei devoted to building a five-storied pagoda. Its setting is modeled on the five-storied pagoda at Yanaka Tennō-ji (commonly known as the Yanaka Five-storied Pagoda). Although that pagoda survived the Great Kantō Earthquake and the Tokyo air raids, it was burned down in July of Shōwa 32 due to an arson-suicide incident ({{ill|Yanaka five-storied pagoda double-suicide arson case|en|Yanaka five-storied pagoda double-suicide arson case|qid=Q8048250|lt=Yanaka Five-storied Pagoda Arson-Suicide Incident|1=Yanaka five-storied pagoda double-suicide arson case}}). * The film {{ill|Game of Death|en|Game of Death|qid=Q854576|lt=Death Game|1=Game of Death}}, starring {{ill|Bruce Lee|en|Bruce Lee|qid=Q16397|lt=Bruce Lee|1=Bruce Lee}}, was based on a plot in which enemies occupy each floor of a five-storied pagoda, forcing the protagonist to fight his way upward. * Among succulent plants (including {{ill|Cactus|en|Cactus|qid=Q14560|lt=cacti|1=Cactus}} and {{ill|Aloe|en|Aloe|qid=Q127134|lt=aloe|1=Aloe}}), within the family {{ill|Liliaceae|en|Liliaceae|qid=Q53480|lt=Liliaceae|1=Liliaceae}} and the genus {{ill|Haoorchia|de|Haoorchia|ja|ハオルチア属|lt=Haoorchia|lt_de=Haoorchia|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Haoorchia|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ハオルシア属|1=Haworthia|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q133983}}, there is a plant known as {{ill|Gojūnotō|de|Gojūnotō|ja|ゴジュウノトウ|lt=Gojūnotō|lt_de=Gojūnotō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gojūnotō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Five-storied Pagoda).
